ranking_notes: This is the narrowest next move that directly follows the positive barrier-and-scoreboard movement from round 36.
It ranks above restore because the latest result stayed close enough to the base that one more seam-level probe is justified.
candidates.hypothesis: Round 36 showed the kind of near-flat but structurally useful signal that is worth following once more on the live PTX anchor: the new control-path cleanup missed the accepted base by only 0.016528 ms, but both barrier and long-scoreboard moved in the right direction. That means the family is probably not exhausted yet; it likely needs one more scheduler-seam nudge that acts more directly on the handoff between `cp_async_wait_group_0`, `__syncthreads()`, and the future-tile refill order. The cleanest next sub-variant is to retime that steady-state seam without changing launch bounds, tile shape, grouped traversal, or export scratch shape.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Residual wait-group and barrier cadence in the PTX hot-band steady-state loop, especially the seam between finishing the current MMA stage and refilling the reused stage buffer for the future tile.
candidates.risk: Moderate. This is still a bounded scheduler retime on the live hot path, but it can easily trade barrier against scoreboard without producing an end-to-end gain.
metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus 24.164272 ms and 24.175471 ms
candidates.stop_condition: Stop if the next retime materially regresses runtime or merely trades barrier for another stall class without preserving the round-36 counter gains.

candidates.hypothesis: The current live branch is only slightly off the accepted base, but the loop should still keep a clean recovery path ranked near the top because the explored scheduler-seam sub-variants are narrow and can flip from near-flat to clearly negative in one step. If the next barrier / handoff retime loses the current positive signal, restoring the exact 489574e PTX anchor is still the correct way to reset the comparison surface.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Known register-limited plateau on the accepted PTX hot-band surface, used here as a clean recovery anchor if the scheduler-seam follow-up loses signal.

metrics_to_recheck: end-to-end median runtime versus the current 24.191999 ms run

candidates.direction_name: Transplant The Half-Panel Register Budget Into The Correct 256x128 Pivot
ranking_notes: Keep this family alive because it still offers the biggest theoretical occupancy upside.
It stays below the live PTX seam family because the current branch still shows incremental signal close to the accepted base.
candidates.hypothesis: The 256x128 family remains the largest visible occupancy swing in repo history, so it should stay alive in the queue even while the live PTX branch keeps producing small scheduler-level signals. It stays third because the current PTX branch is close enough to the accepted base that one more bounded live-branch probe is more defensible than another broad correctness-risky geometry jump.
candidates.expected_bottleneck: Register footprint and synchronization strategy on the 256x128 hot-band path, plus correctness-sensitive writer ownership.
candidates.risk: High. This remains the broadest and most correctness-sensitive active family.
candidates.stop_condition: Stop immediately on correctness failure or on another 28-30 ms class regression without a decisive occupancy breakthrough.
